title: Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita, 2023

Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between economic output and health outcomes for countries in 2023, plotting DALYs from non-communicable diseases per 100,000 people on the vertical axis against GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic horizontal axis. Circles sized by population distinguish the scale of each nation, while color coding identifies regions including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data points form a downward-sloping distribution, showing that higher income levels coincide with lower disease burdens from non-communicable conditions. At the upper end of the disease burden scale, Nauru, the Solomon Islands, and the Marshall Islands sit above 40,000 DALYs per 100,000, with GDP per capita figures generally below $15,000. In the middle of the chart, countries like India, Pakistan, and Myanmar cluster between 20,000 and 30,000 DALYs, while Egypt and Turkmenistan appear slightly lower in burden but remain in the lower-middle income range. High-income nations occupy the bottom right quadrant; Singapore, Japan, Germany, and the United States all record disease burdens below 20,000 DALYs per 100,000, with GDP per capita extending toward or beyond $50,000. Large population centers like China and Indonesia sit in the lower-middle band of the chart, visually distinct due to their circle size. The visual arrangement confirms a negative correlation where wealthier economies consistently display reduced disability-adjusted life years lost to non-communicable diseases compared to lower-income counterparts.
